report_name: APT28 'FrostArmada' Campaign Hijacks SOHO Routers for Global DNS Espionage

threat_actors.name: APT28
threat_actors.description: A Russian state-sponsored threat group identified as the actor behind the 'FrostArmada' campaign. The group compromises SOHO routers on a large scale to perform DNS hijacking for global cyber espionage and credential harvesting.
threat_actors.goals: To harvest authentication credentials and other sensitive data from government agencies, law enforcement, and cloud service users for intelligence gathering purposes.

attack_patterns.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
attack_patterns.description: APT28 exploited known vulnerabilities in the web administration interfaces of MikroTik and TP-Link SOHO routers that were exposed to the internet to gain initial access.

attack_patterns.name: Default Accounts
attack_patterns.description: APT28 gained initial access to SOHO routers by exploiting weak or default administrative credentials on their web interfaces.

attack_patterns.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle
attack_patterns.description: The threat actor modified the DNS settings on compromised routers to point to their own malicious servers, allowing them to perform Adversary-in-the-Middle (AitM) attacks to intercept traffic and harvest credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Network Sniffing
attack_patterns.description: The attacker's Adversary-in-the-Middle (AitM) nodes passively captured all traffic from victims, including credentials, session cookies, and other sensitive data.

campaigns.name: FrostArmada
campaigns.description: A large-scale, long-running cyber espionage campaign attributed to APT28, active since at least May 2025. The campaign involves the mass compromise of insecure SOHO routers to perform DNS hijacking for credential harvesting and data collection on a global scale.
campaigns.objective: Cyber espionage against government, law enforcement, and cloud service users.

identities.name: MikroTik
identities.description: Manufacturer of SOHO routers that were exploited by APT28 in the FrostArmada campaign.

identities.name: TP-Link
identities.description: Manufacturer of SOHO routers that were exploited by APT28 in the FrostArmada campaign.

identities.name: Government Agencies
identities.description: A primary class of victims targeted by the FrostArmada campaign for intelligence gathering.

identities.name: Law Enforcement
identities.description: A class of organizations targeted for credential harvesting in the FrostArmada campaign.

identities.name: Cloud Service Users
identities.description: A class of victims targeted by the FrostArmada campaign to intercept cloud service credentials.

infrastructure.name: FrostArmada SOHO Router Botnet
infrastructure.description: A distributed network of compromised MikroTik and TP-Link SOHO routers used by APT28 to hijack DNS traffic from victims.

infrastructure.name: FrostArmada DNS Hijacking Servers
infrastructure.description: Malicious DNS servers controlled by APT28 that provide IP addresses of AitM servers in response to queries from compromised routers.

infrastructure.name: FrostArmada AitM Credential Harvesting Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Attacker-in-the-Middle (AitM) servers that intercept victim traffic, harvest credentials, and passively collect data.

indicators.name: Anomalous DNS Server Configuration
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Outbound DNS Queries to Unauthorized Servers
id: 7e8b1c2a-3d4e-4f5a-8b9c-0d1e2f3a4b5c
status: experimental
description: |
  Detects DNS queries sent to DNS servers not on an approved list. This can be an indicator of DNS hijacking, where a compromised device (like a SOHO router in the FrostArmada campaign) redirects DNS traffic to a malicious resolver for Adversary-in-the-Middle attacks.
references:
  - https://blog.lumen.com/frostarmada-apt28-compromises-soho-routers/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-08
tags:
  - attack.t1557
  - attack.credential_access
level: medium
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate use of third-party DNS resolvers by users or software.
  - Misconfigured network settings.
logsource:
  category: dns
detection:
  selection:
    # This selection should be adapted to your environment's logging capabilities for the DNS server that handled the query.
    # The logic is to detect queries to any server that is NOT in the allowed list.
    # As Sigma lacks a standard field for the destination DNS server, this rule is conceptual.
    # A practical implementation would be in a SIEM monitoring firewall logs for outbound traffic on port 53/udp to non-approved IPs.
    - c-ip: '1.1.1.1' # Placeholder for a SIEM field representing the destination IP of the DNS query
  filter:
    c-ip:
      - '8.8.8.8'    # Google DNS
      - '8.8.4.4'    # Google DNS
      - '1.1.1.1'    # Cloudflare DNS
      - '1.0.0.1'    # Cloudflare DNS
      - '9.9.9.9'    # Quad9 DNS
      - '208.67.222.222' # OpenDNS
      - '192.168.1.1' # Example internal DNS
  condition: selection and not filter
indicators.description: Detects when client devices make DNS queries to unauthorized DNS servers, which is a key indicator of the DNS hijacking technique used in the FrostArmada campaign.
